63e3f13e1843aa59132015b7.question: In a chapter devoted to these things in Towards a New Architecture, Le Corbusier hailed them as "the magnificent first fruits of the New Age". Oliver Evan's 18th century predecessor to these things was a hand-powered mechanism with a lot of iron buckets. The first modern device of this kind was developed in Buffalo by Joseph Dart, who introduced a steam engine into these things, reducing loading times by an order of magnitude. One of these structures owned by John W. Eshelman & Sons in Lancaster, Pennsylvania is depicted in (*) Charles Demuth's painting My Egypt. Article 13 of an 1869 state constitution made all of these structures public entities and required government permission to mix different grades within them. The aforementioned bill led to the case of Munn v. Illinois. For 10 points, name these buildings that lift and store wheat and corn.

For 10 points, name these devices placed on one's feet to move around namesake rinks.

After reluctantly agreeing to go into his father’s business for one year, he secured a profitable share in a venture at Stone Bluff. One structure commissioned by this man was modeled on the Villa of the Papyri in Herculaneum. This legendary cheapskate made guests at his Sutton Place mansion in London use a payphone. This man imagined the title person being able to “walk and talk with the great Greek philosophers” in his book, The Joys of Collecting. His celebrity rose in 1957 when Fortune declared him America’s (*) richest man. An actor playing this man was changed late in the production of All the Money in the World. Richard Meier designed a multi-building campus to house the extensive art collection his namesake Trust purchased. For 10 points, name this oil magnate and namesake of a celebrated Los Angeles-area art museum.

63e3f13e1843aa59132015e5.question: Ted Marvin Shinault is the only member of a police force for these institutions who has died in the line of duty. The plot of a 1976 movie titled for these things gets going when Harry Lucas, played by Jim Hutton, has a mishap involving a bag of homemade fudge. That film was titled "Who's Minding [one of these institutions]?" The first of these places was the first building erected by orders of the US government and was supervised by David Rittenhouse in Philadelphia. Charles E. Barber served as its Chief (*) Engraver. A celebrated item from one of these institutions made from 1907 to 1933 was designed by Augustus Saint-Gaudens. A now-closed organization of this type in Dahlonega was founded in 1835; Dahlonega’s “D” mark shouldn’t be confused with the “D” from the Denver one, established during the Pikes Peak Gold Rush. For 10 points, name these institutions which literally make money.

It doesn’t have to do with bicycles, but Peloton Technology is looking improve the efficiency of people in this profession through the use of platooning. A former startup looking to put these people out of work may have been helped by Anthony (*) Levandowski stealing a bunch of trade secrets from his former employer. Problems with a satellite link to France disrupted a "space yodel" this man planned for an event hosted by George Plimpton on New Year's Day in 1984. Charlotte Moorman was an assistant to this man. He placed a magnet on top of his signature devices to generate abstract forms. In his report "Media Planning for the Postindustrial Society" he coined the term "electronic (*) super highway". He had a breakthrough moment during Pope Paul VI's arrival in New York City, aided by his recently purchased Sony Portapak. Scenes from The Wizard of Oz play in the Kansas screen of one of his works, which consists of a map of the US made from some 300 TV screens. For 10 points, name this Korean-American video artist.

In 1971, it became the first work of its type to win a National Book Award. This thing was divided into sections such as “Community” and “Nomadics”. In another deadly repeat, a mob in Quito set fire to a building and killed a pianist and violinist involved in orchestrating it. A. Brad Schwartz's history of this event argues that newspapers deliberately played up reactions to it to discredit rival businesses. Grover's Mill steelworker Bill Dock "didn't see anybody he thought needed shooting" during this event.
